topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the reception hall felt thick, suffocating. Every forced smile from the crowd seemed like a physical assault. I watched from across the room, a tight knot forming in my chest, every muscle tense. Then, across the dance floor, there he was. Liam. He was laughing, looking impossibly happy, his arm draped around someone else. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I took a sharp breath, the scent of expensive perfume suddenly overwhelming, and I made a swift, deliberate exit toward the balcony, needing air that didn't taste like this manufactured joy.

[story 2]

I kept my hands gripping my champagne flute so tightly the crystal threatened to crack. The music was too loud, the laughter too bright, and then my eyes locked onto him. He was standing near the buffet, looking effortlessly charming, and the sudden jolt of memory hit me like a physical blow. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I felt a sudden, intense need to disappear. I deliberately turned my back to the scene, focusing instead on the intricate pattern of the wallpaper. Every glance I tried to avoid sent a tremor through my hands, making me want to shove the glass across the table.

[story 3]

When I saw him, everything seemed to slow down, the ambient noise of the celebration receding into a dull hum. It was like a switch flipped inside my head, plunging everything into a cold, sharp clarity. I watched him approach, and a strange, brittle energy began to vibrate beneath my skin. I didn't move, just stared, my gaze unwavering, challenging the polite veneer of the evening. When he finally turned, a flicker of something unreadable crossed his face, and I felt a fierce, internal tightening—a pressure building behind my eyes that demanded release. I took a slow, deliberate sip of my drink, letting the silence between us stretch taut.

I watched from across the room, a tight knot forming in my chest, every muscle tense. Then, across the dance floor, there he was. Liam. I took a sharp breath, the scent of expensive perfume suddenly overwhelming, and I made a swift, deliberate exit toward the balcony, needing air that didn't taste like this manufactured joy.

Every glance I tried to avoid sent a tremor through my hands, making me want to shove the glass across the table.

When I saw him, everything seemed to slow down, the ambient noise of the celebration receding into a dull hum. It was like a switch flipped inside my head, plunging everything into a cold, sharp clarity. I watched him approach, and a strange, brittle energy began to vibrate beneath my skin. I didn't move, just stared, my gaze unwavering, challenging the polite veneer of the evening. When he finally turned, a flicker of something unreadable crossed his face, and I felt a fierce, internal tightening—a pressure building behind my eyes that demanded release. I took a slow, deliberate sip of my drink, letting the silence between us stretch taut.
